skill.name: writing-skills
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through test-driven creation, editing, and verification of AI skills.
skill.summary: Create and verify AI skills with a test-driven documentation workflow.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but manual review found most are false positives from Markdown examples, image URLs, and documentation keywords. The real risk is moderate: the skill can guide agents toward local skill-directory writes, subagent testing, and Git push workflows if users follow the checklist. No evidence found of malicious intent, credential theft, hidden execution payloads, prompt injection attempts, or data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Operational Guidance Can Modify Local Files and Publish Changes
medium_findings.description: The skill states that personal skills are written to ~/.claude/skills and includes a deployment checklist item to commit and push to a fork. This is legitimate for skill authoring, but it can affect local files and remote repositories if followed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The operational instructions are explicit and semantically relevant. Risk remains moderate because they are checklist guidance, not hidden automatic execution.

medium_findings.title: Agent Compliance Techniques Require Human Review
medium_findings.description: The supporting persuasion reference recommends authority and commitment patterns for discipline-enforcing skills. The same file includes ethical-use limits, so this is not malicious, but it can make skill instructions more forceful.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file openly describes compliance techniques and provides ethical boundaries. It is a legitimate documentation topic, but marketplace reviewers should verify user-serving intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Mistaken for Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external-command alerts occur inside fenced Markdown examples, inline command names, or instructional reference text. They are not executable code paths and do not run by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations are documentation examples and prose, not scripts. No executable wrapper or automatic invocation was found in these files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Image Links
low_findings.description: Network alerts point to image URLs embedded in Anthropic best-practice documentation. They are static reference links and do not transmit local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are img src and srcset values in copied reference material. There is no fetch call, webhook, credential value, or exfiltration flow.

low_findings.title: Keyword-Based Blocker Alerts Lack Malicious Context
low_findings.description: Weak-crypto, C2, and reconnaissance alerts are caused by ordinary words and example file names in documentation. No encryption routine, command-and-control endpoint, or reconnaissance workflow was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is style guidance, example names, and research background. Manual review did not find malware semantics behind the keyword matches.

low_findings.title: Reference File Write Example Is Non-Operational
low_findings.description: The Python file-write alert appears in Anthropic guidance explaining how utility scripts should handle missing files. This skill does not include that script as an executable tool.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is inside a Markdown reference example. No packaged script or command path invokes it.

content.user_title: Create and Test AI Writing Skills
content.value_statement: AI skills often fail because instructions are written before real behavior is tested. This skill applies test-driven development to skill writing so agents verify behavior before deployment.
actual_capabilities: Defines a RED-GREEN-REFACTOR workflow for creating and editing skills.
Explains how to test skill behavior with pressure scenarios and subagents.
Provides guidance for skill descriptions, naming, keywords, and file organization.
Includes official Anthropic skill authoring guidance as supporting reference material.
Documents Graphviz conventions for small decision flowcharts.
Explains ethical persuasion patterns for discipline-enforcing skill instructions.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not provide executable validation scripts.
It assumes the user understands test-driven development concepts.
It focuses on Claude-style skills, not every agent framework.
It can recommend Git and filesystem actions that need user review.

use_cases.title: Create a New Skill
use_cases.description: Use the RED-GREEN-REFACTOR process to test agent failure modes before writing a new reusable skill.

use_cases.title: Improve an Existing Skill
use_cases.description: Use pressure scenarios and rationalization tables to close gaps in a skill that agents misapply.

use_cases.title: Review Skill Quality
use_cases.description: Check descriptions, examples, flowcharts, and deployment steps before publishing a skill to a team marketplace.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Skill Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a new AI skill for [task]. Identify trigger conditions, expected behavior, and one failing baseline scenario.

prompt_templates.title: Test Before Writing
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three pressure scenarios for a skill about [practice]. Predict how an agent might rationalize skipping the practice.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this skill draft for loopholes, vague triggers, weak examples, and missing verification steps. Suggest precise revisions.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare for Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this completed skill for metadata quality, progressive disclosure, testing evidence, and safe deployment steps.

output_examples.input: Create a skill for avoiding flaky async tests.
output_examples.output: A concise skill outline with trigger conditions, baseline failure scenarios, required test behavior, and verification criteria.

output_examples.input: Review my skill description for search quality.
output_examples.output: A revised description that starts with use conditions, names symptoms clearly, and includes searchable tool and workflow terms.

output_examples.input: Find gaps in this discipline-enforcing skill.
output_examples.output: A rationalization table, red flags, missing counters, and targeted edits that make the instruction harder to bypass.

best_practices: Run a failing baseline scenario before writing or editing the skill.
Keep the main skill concise and move heavy reference material to supporting files.
Use concrete trigger conditions, examples, and verification steps.
anti_patterns: Writing a skill from intuition without observing baseline agent behavior.
Adding broad narrative history that future agents cannot reuse.
Creating many weak examples instead of one strong, adaptable example.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps authors create AI skills that are tested against real agent behavior before deployment.

faq.question: Does it generate skills automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides a process for planning, testing, writing, and verifying skill documentation.

faq.question: Can Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks it compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include executable scripts?
faq.answer: No evidence found of bundled executable scripts. The files are Markdown, Dot reference material, and an archive.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention filesystem and Git risk?
faq.answer: The skill discusses writing personal skills and committing changes. Users should review paths and Git commands before acting.

faq.question: Is the persuasion guidance safe?
faq.answer: It is framed for ethical documentation and discipline enforcement, but reviewers should confirm it serves the user interest.
